title: Optimization: Argument Register Reuse Before First Call
description:
## Summary
Allow argument registers (X0-X7) to be used directly in the first basic block before saving to callee-saved registers.

## Problem
Dark moves all arguments to callee-saved registers immediately on function entry, even when they could be used directly for the first operation.

## Evidence from tak Benchmark

**Rust approach:**
```asm
cmp    x0, x1       ; DIRECT use of argument registers
mov    x19, x2      ; Only save z (might need it)
b.ls   .LBB4_3      ; Early exit - no need to save x, y
mov    x20, x1      ; Save y (only if not taking early exit)
mov    x21, x0      ; Save x
```

**Dark approach:**
```
X21 <- Mov(Reg X0)    ; Save x first
X20 <- Mov(Reg X1)    ; Save y first
X19 <- Mov(Reg X2)    ; Save z first
...
Cmp(X24, Reg X23)     ; Compare saved copies, not originals
```

Rust uses the argument registers directly for the comparison, avoiding unnecessary moves in the early-exit path.

## Solution
1. Track when argument registers are still valid (not yet clobbered by a call)
2. Allow comparison and arithmetic operations to use argument registers directly
3. Only save to callee-saved when:
   - A function call is about to be made
   - The value is needed after a loop back edge

This requires interaction between MIR generation and register allocation.

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/3_ANF_to_MIR.fs - Generate better CFG structure with delayed saves
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/5_RegisterAllocation.fs - Add argument register preference

## Estimated Impact
1-2% speedup on functions with early-exit paths

title: Optimization: Redundant Move Elimination in Register Allocator
description:
## Summary
Eliminate redundant move instructions generated during register allocation and phi resolution.

## Impact
~40-50% performance improvement across all benchmarks

## Evidence
From mandelbrot LIR after register allocation:
```
D0 <- FMov(D5)
D0 <- FMov(D6)    // D0 overwritten, previous write wasted
D0 <- FMov(D1)    // D0 overwritten again
D0 <- FMov(D4)    // D0 overwritten again
X1 <- Mov(Reg X2)
X1 <- Mov(Reg X3) // X1 overwritten, previous write wasted
```

Also from iterate function (shuffling values unnecessarily):
```
D7 <- FMov(D0)
D4 <- FMov(D1)
D6 <- FMov(D2)
D5 <- FMov(D3)
```

Self-moves that have no effect:
```
X2 <- Mov(Reg X2)  // Self-move, no-op
X3 <- Mov(Reg X3)  // Self-move, no-op
D1 <- FMov(D1)     // Self-move, no-op
```

These redundant moves account for ~50% of instructions in hot loops.

## Implementation Approach
1. Add post-register-allocation dead store elimination
2. Eliminate self-moves (X2 <- Mov(X2))
3. Detect and remove overwritten writes (consecutive writes to same destination)
4. Improve move coalescing during phi resolution

## Files to Modify
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/5_RegisterAllocation.fs` - Improve phi coalescing
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/6_CodeGen.fs` - Peephole optimization
- Consider `src/DarkCompiler/passes/5.5_PostRA_Optimize.fs` - New pass for cleanup

## Related Investigation
docs/investigations/benchmark-spectral_norm-optimization.md
docs/investigations/benchmark-leibniz-optimization.md (same issue)

title: Repeatedly fix issues in benchmarks
description: Determine which benchmarks are not working, and for each broken benchmark, add a task to fix the compiler such that the benchmark works. The task should investigate why the benchmark does not work, and add sub-tasks to fix each of those issues. The task is not finished until its subtasks have fully fixed the benchmark to work.

comments.author: Paul Biggar
comments.text: Investigated broken benchmarks. Found and fixed list literal truncation bug (c4d-2dg.2). Quicksort benchmark now compiles and runs with small inputs (100-300 elements). Segfaults on larger inputs (500+) due to stack overflow - created separate issue c4d-2g8 for that. Other benchmarks still need investigation but this iteration fixed a major issue.

title: Fix list literal truncation at 6+ elements
description: List literals with more than 5 elements are truncated because the compiler only supports prefix (1 element) + suffix (4 elements). The middle spine for FingerTree is not implemented. Need to build list using push operations for larger lists or implement proper FingerTree construction.

comments.author: Paul Biggar
comments.text: Fixed list literal truncation. Lists with 6+ elements were being truncated because the compiler only supported prefix (1 element) + suffix (4 elements = max). Now use pushBack operations to build larger lists. Tested with 8-element lists and quicksort benchmark with 100-300 elements successfully.

title: Fix stack overflow on deep recursion (quicksort fails at 500+ elements)
description: Quicksort benchmark works with 100-300 elements but segfaults with 500+ elements due to stack overflow from deep recursion. Need to investigate: 1) TCO (tail call optimization) not being applied to some recursive calls 2) Stack space limits in generated code 3) Consider trampolining or iterative approaches for deeply recursive benchmarks
close_reason: Documented limitation: stdlib FingerTree operations cause stack overflow with large lists (>1000 elements). Fixing requires significant architectural changes to stdlib.

comments.author: Paul Biggar
comments.text:
Root cause analysis complete:

1. TCO (tail call optimization) IS working correctly for simple recursive functions
2. The issue is with stdlib FingerTree operations (tail, head, filter, etc.) which:
   - Are not tail-recursive internally
   - Make many nested function calls
   - When called thousands of times (once per list element), exhaust the stack

3. Specific problematic functions:
   - Stdlib.FingerTree.tail - uses __tailOfNode which recurses to tree depth
   - Pattern matching [h, ...t] - internally uses head/tail
   - Stdlib.List.filter - iterates calling head/tail for each element

4. The ~1100 iteration limit comes from stack space exhaustion when repeatedly calling these stdlib functions

Solutions would require either:
- Increasing stack size at runtime
- Rewriting stdlib functions to use loops/trampolining
- Different list implementation

For now, this is a known limitation: lists larger than ~1000 elements may cause stack overflow when using iteration patterns.

title: Optimization: Hardware fsqrt for Integer Square Root
description:
## Summary
Use hardware floating-point sqrt instruction for integer square root calculations instead of recursive computation.

## Impact Estimate
30-40% performance improvement on primes benchmark (and similar benchmarks using sqrt).

## Root Cause
Dark computes `isqrt(n, 1)` using recursive trial-and-error, requiring O(sqrt(n)) iterations per prime check. For n=10000, this means up to 100 recursive calls per isqrt computation.

### Evidence from Dark ANF (isqrt hot loop):
```
Function isqrt:
let TempId 2 = t1 * t1              ; guess * guess
let TempId 3 = t2 > t0              ; > n?
if t3 then
    let TempId 4 = t1 - 1
    return t4
else
    let TempId 5 = t1 + 1           ; computed THREE times
    let TempId 6 = t1 + 1           ; REDUNDANT
    let TempId 7 = t5 * t6
    let TempId 8 = t7 > t0
    if t8 then
        return t1
    else
        let TempId 9 = t1 + 1       ; REDUNDANT (third time)
        let TempId 10 = TailCall(isqrt, [t0, t9])
```

### Rust comparison (3 instructions total):
```asm
ucvtf d0, x12       ; convert int to double
fsqrt d0, d0        ; SINGLE hardware instruction (~12 cycles)
fcvtzs x13, d0      ; convert back to int
```

### Instruction count comparison:
- Dark: ~11 instructions per iteration, O(sqrt(n)) iterations = ~1100 instructions for n=10000
- Rust: 3 instructions total

## Implementation Approach
1. Add `Int64.sqrt` builtin that emits ARM64 sequence: `ucvtf`, `fsqrt`, `fcvtzs`
2. Add type checking for the new builtin
3. Consider pattern-matching existing isqrt recursion patterns for automatic replacement

## Files to Modify
- `src/codegen/lir_to_arm64.rs` - Add fsqrt emission
- `src/stdlib/` - Add Int64.sqrt builtin
- `src/types/` - Type checking for sqrt

## Reference
See full investigation: docs/investigations/benchmark-primes-optimization.md

title: Fix: Segmentation fault in checksumHelper empty list handling
description:
## Critical Bug

The quicksort benchmark crashes with SIGSEGV (exit code 139) when running.

### Root Cause

From the ANF IR analysis, there's a null pointer dereference in the checksumHelper function:

```
Function checksumHelper:
let TempId 1319 = t1316 == 0
if t1319 then return t1318
else
    let TempId 1321 = t1316 != 0
    if t1321 then
        let TempId 1322 = t1316 & 7              ; Extract tag
        let TempId 1323 = t1316 & -8             ; Extract pointer
        ...
    else
        let TempId 1320 = RawGet(0, 0)           ; DEREFERENCE NULL!
        return t1320
```

The `else` branch explicitly dereferences address 0, causing a guaranteed crash.

### Evidence

- Exit code 139 = SIGSEGV
- `RawGet(0, 0)` appears in the generated ANF for checksumHelper
- The pattern match on list (`[]` vs `[h, ...t]`) is not properly handling the empty list case

### Implementation Approach

1. Fix empty list handling in match desugaring
   - Ensure `[]` pattern correctly handles null/0 representation
   - Add null check before RawGet operations

2. Add runtime bounds checking (debug mode)
   - Validate pointer before dereference
   - Better error messages for null pointer access

### Files to Modify

- `src/desugar/pattern_match.rs` - Fix empty list case
- `src/codegen/` - Add null check emission

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Quicksort benchmark runs without crashing
- [ ] All list pattern matches handle empty lists correctly
- [ ] Test case added for empty list pattern matching

title: Fix quicksort bug in benchmarks
description: It appears to be a closure capture bug

comments.author: Paul Biggar
comments.text: Fixed closure capture bug. Root cause: simpleInferType didn't handle AST.Call expressions, so variables bound to function call results weren't added to TypeEnv and couldn't be captured by closures. Fix: Added FuncReturnTypes map to LiftState to track function return types, updated simpleInferType to look up return types for Call and TypeApp expressions. Quicksort now compiles (has runtime segfault - separate issue). All 3401 tests pass.

title: Optimization: Constant Float Loading/Hoisting
description:
## Summary
Optimize constant float handling: hoist invariant loads out of loops and use FMOV immediate encoding for common constants.

## Impact
~5-10% performance improvement for numerical code

## Evidence
From Dark mandelbrot LIR - same constant loaded twice in same block:
```
fv1000 <- FLoad(float[2])        // Load 2.0 from memory
fv10083 <- FMul(fv1000, fv10081)
...
fv1000 <- FLoad(float[2])        // Load 2.0 AGAIN in same block
fv10086 <- FMul(fv1000, fv10082)
```

Rust keeps constants in registers throughout hot loops:
```asm
9390: 6f03f600    fmov v0.2d, #1.0   // Immediate encoding, no memory access
// d0 kept as 1.0 throughout entire hot loop
```

ARM64 FMOV supports immediate encoding for common constants: 0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, etc.

## Implementation Approach
1. Add loop-invariant code motion (LICM) pass to hoist constant loads
2. Use ARM64 FMOV immediate encoding for common constants
3. CSE for constant loads within a basic block

## Files to Modify
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/3.5_MIR_Optimize.fs` - Add LICM pass
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/6_CodeGen.fs` - FMOV immediate for common constants
- `src/DarkCompiler/ARM64.fs` - FMOV immediate encoding if not present

## Related Investigation
docs/investigations/benchmark-spectral_norm-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Inline trivial closures and hoist closure allocations
description:
## Optimization: Closure Allocation Optimization

**Impact Estimate:** 15-20% improvement for filter-heavy code

### Problem

Dark allocates closures inside loops for each filter call:

**Dark quicksort ANF:**
```
Function quicksort:
let TempId 1284 = getAtOrDefault(t1279, t1283, 0)      ; pivot
let TempId 1286 = ClosureAlloc(__closure_0, [t1284])   ; ALLOC for x < pivot
let TempId 1287 = Stdlib.List.filter_i64(t1279, t1286)
let TempId 1289 = ClosureAlloc(__closure_1, [t1284])   ; ALLOC for x == pivot
let TempId 1290 = Stdlib.List.filter_i64(t1279, t1289)
let TempId 1292 = ClosureAlloc(__closure_2, [t1284])   ; ALLOC for x > pivot
let TempId 1293 = Stdlib.List.filter_i64(t1279, t1292)
```

### Evidence

The three closure types have trivial bodies that could be inlined:
```
Function __closure_0:
let TempId 2 = t0.1            ; load pivot from closure environment
let TempId 4 = t1 < t2         ; compare: element < pivot
return t4
```

**Rust approach (inline comparison):**
```asm
96ec:   eb15027f    cmp x19, x21    ; direct register comparison
96f0:   54ffff8a    b.ge 96e4       ; branch
```

Rust keeps the pivot in a register (x21) throughout the filter loop - no closure allocation or indirect call needed.

### Implementation Approach

1. **Closure Inlining Pass**
   - Detect closures with single primitive operations
   - Inline the comparison at call sites
   - Eliminate closure allocation entirely

2. **Filter Fusion**
   - Combine multiple filters over same list into single traversal
   - `filter(arr, x < p) + filter(arr, x == p) + filter(arr, x > p)` → `partition3(arr, p)`

### Files to Modify

- `src/anf/optimizations/closure_inline.rs` - Inline trivial closures
- `src/anf/optimizations/filter_fusion.rs` - Combine consecutive filters

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Trivial closures (single comparison) are inlined at call sites
- [ ] Closure allocation eliminated for inlined closures
- [ ] Consider: Filter fusion for partition patterns

title: LIR: consolidate optimizations as peephole pass + rename

title: Optimization: Add Array/Vector Type Support
description:
## Summary
Add array/vector type support to enable implementation of spectral_norm and other array-based benchmarks.

## Impact
**BLOCKER** for spectral_norm benchmark - currently Dark can only implement a simplified version that computes matA(0,0) instead of the full algorithm.

## Evidence
Current Dark implementation is reduced to:
```dark
let row0 = matA(0, 0) in
let scaled = Stdlib.Float.toInt(row0 * 1000000000.0) in
scaled
```

The full algorithm requires three vectors of size n=100:
```rust
let mut u = vec![1.0; n];
let mut v = vec![0.0; n];
let mut tmp = vec![0.0; n];
```

Rust achieves efficient array access with single instructions:
```asm
fc6b7a63    ldr d3, [x19, x11, lsl #3]  // v[j] - indexed load
fc297801    str d1, [x0, x9, lsl #3]    // out[i] = s - indexed store
```

## Implementation Approach
1. Add array type to AST: `TArray of DType`
2. Add array literals: `[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]`
3. Add indexed access: `arr[i]`
4. Add array mutation: `arr[i] = x`
5. Implement heap allocation for arrays in runtime

## Files to Modify
- `src/DarkCompiler/AST.fs` - Add array type
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/1_Parser.fs` - Parse array syntax
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/1.5_TypeChecking.fs` - Type check arrays
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/2_AST_to_ANF.fs` - ANF for array ops
- `src/DarkCompiler/Runtime.fs` - Heap allocation

## Related Investigation
docs/investigations/benchmark-spectral_norm-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Redundant Move Elimination in Register Allocator
description:
## Summary
The leibniz benchmark shows Dark is ~17x slower than Rust. Analysis reveals the register allocator is emitting many redundant moves that survive through code generation, accounting for 10 out of ~17 instructions in the hot loop.

## Evidence from LIR (After Register Allocation)
The hot loop in leibniz shows:
```
X1 <- Mov(Reg X2)
X1 <- Mov(Reg X3)        // X1 overwritten, previous write wasted
D0 <- FMov(D1)
D0 <- FMov(D2)           // D0 overwritten, previous write wasted
X2 <- Mov(Reg X2)        // Self-move, no effect
X3 <- Mov(Reg X3)        // Self-move, no effect
D1 <- FMov(D1)           // Self-move, no effect
D0 <- FMov(D2)
D2 <- FMov(D0)           // Move chain D2→D0→D2
```

These account for 10 of ~17 instructions, making the loop over 2x larger than necessary.

## Root Cause
1. Self-moves (reg to same reg) are not being eliminated
2. Overwritten writes are not being detected/eliminated
3. Move coalescing is not eliminating enough copies during phi resolution

## Impact
Estimated 40-50% performance improvement

## Implementation Approach
1. Add a post-register-allocation pass to eliminate trivial moves
2. Improve phi coalescing in register allocation to reduce move generation
3. Add dead store elimination for overwrites (write followed by another write to same destination)

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/5_RegisterAllocation.fs - Improve phi coalescing
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/6_CodeGen.fs - Strengthen peephole optimizer
- Consider adding src/DarkCompiler/passes/5.5_PostRA_Optimize.fs - Post-RA cleanup

## Benchmark Reference
See: docs/investigations/benchmark-leibniz-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Replace FingerTree with cons-list for sequential list operations
description:
## Optimization: Simplify List Data Structure for Sequential Access

**Impact Estimate:** 70-80% reduction in overhead for filter/map operations

### Problem

Dark uses a sophisticated FingerTree data structure for lists, which provides O(1) amortized append and O(log n) random access. However, for the quicksort benchmark, the operations are primarily sequential:
- `filter`: Sequential traversal (O(n))
- `head/tail`: List decomposition

The FingerTree adds massive overhead for these operations.

### Evidence from IR Analysis

**Dark filterHelper ANF shows complex operations:**
```
Function Stdlib.List.__filterHelper_i64:
let TempId 1181 = Stdlib.FingerTree.isEmpty_i64(t1178)
...
    let TempId 1182 = Stdlib.FingerTree.head_i64(t1178)    ; COMPLEX: tag checks, node traversal
    ...
    let TempId 1188 = Stdlib.FingerTree.pushBack_i64(t1180, t1185)  ; COMPLEX: rebalancing
    ...
    let TempId 1191 = Stdlib.FingerTree.tail_i64(t1178)  ; VERY COMPLEX: ~400 lines of ANF
```

The `tail_i64` function spans ~400 lines of ANF because it handles:
- Single nodes, deep nodes with prefix/suffix/middle components
- Rebalancing when prefix is exhausted
- Node explosion and reconstruction

**Rust approach (tight loop):**
```asm
96e0:   f84086f3    ldr x19, [x23], #8    ; load element (post-increment)
96ec:   eb15027f    cmp x19, x21          ; compare to pivot
96f0:   54ffff8a    b.ge 96e4             ; branch
```

### Instruction Count Estimate

Per filter element:
- Dark (FingerTree): ~85 operations
- Rust (Vec iterator): ~6 operations
- OCaml (cons-list): ~15 operations

For quicksort on 5000 elements: Dark would execute ~14x more instructions than Rust for filter operations alone.

### Implementation Options

**Option A: Specialize filter for sequential iteration**
- Detect when `filter` is the only consumer of a list
- Use a simple iterator that walks the tree without reconstructing
- Build result directly as contiguous array

**Option B: Add simple cons-list type**
- `SimpleList<T>` with head/tail representation
- O(1) cons, head, tail operations
- Convert from FingerTree only when random access needed

**Option C: Inline FingerTree operations for common cases**
- Specialize `tail` for single-element prefix (most common)
- Eliminate tag checks when type is statically known

### Files to Modify

- `src/stdlib/list.dark` - Add optimized filter implementation
- `src/anf/optimizations/` - Add list operation specialization
- Consider: `src/stdlib/simple_list.dark` - Lightweight list type

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Filter operation uses O(n) simple operations instead of O(n * log n) tree operations
- [ ] Quicksort benchmark runs within 10x of OCaml performance

title: Optimization: Phi Resolution Move Optimization
description:
## Summary
When converting out of SSA form, phi nodes generate excessive moves, especially for loop-invariant values.

## Evidence from MIR
The leibniz hot loop shows:
```
v16 <- v11 : TInt64      // Copy v11 to v16
v17 <- v1 : TInt64       // Copy v1 to v17 (but v1 is loop-invariant!)
v18 <- v12 : TFloat64
v19 <- v13 : TFloat64
v0 <- v16 : TInt64       // Copy v16 to v0
v1 <- v17 : TInt64       // Copy v17 to v1 (copying back the same value!)
v2 <- v18 : TFloat64
v3 <- v19 : TFloat64
```

The loop-invariant value `v1` (the loop bound `n`) is being copied through intermediate registers unnecessarily. This generates 4 moves when 0 are needed.

## Root Cause
1. Phi resolution doesn't detect loop-invariant operands
2. Parallel move sequences use more temporaries than needed
3. ParallelMoves.fs may not be fully utilized

## Impact
Estimated 20-30% performance improvement

## Implementation Approach
1. In phi resolution, detect when a phi operand is loop-invariant and skip the move
2. Optimize parallel move sequences to use fewer temporaries
3. Consider using `ParallelMoves.fs` more effectively to minimize move count

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/4_MIR_to_LIR.fs - Phi resolution
- src/DarkCompiler/ParallelMoves.fs - Move sequence optimization

## Benchmark Reference
See: docs/investigations/benchmark-leibniz-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Specialize small list access patterns
description:
## Problem
`getAt` for small lists (size < 4) goes through full FingerTree dispatch machinery, which is excessive overhead for tiny lists.

## Evidence from LIR dump (matmul benchmark)

For a 3-element list, the access path is:
```
Stdlib.FingerTree.getAt_i64:
  Cmp(v551, Imm 0)           ; negative index check
  Branch...
  And_imm(v550, #7)          ; extract tag
  Call(__TAG_SINGLE)         ; is it a SINGLE node?
  Cmp(v11328, Reg v11330)
  Branch...
  Call(__TAG_DEEP)           ; is it a DEEP node?
  Cmp(v11328, Reg v11334)    
  Branch...
  Call(__toRawPtr_i64)       ; get raw pointer
  Call(__DEEP_MEASURE_OFF)   ; get measure offset
  RawGet(v11340, v11341)     ; load measure
  ; ... continues with prefix/middle/suffix dispatch
```

For a list known at compile time to have 3 elements, this should be a single indexed load.

## Solution
1. Track list sizes at compile time when possible (list literals, known transformations)
2. Specialize `getAt` for small known-size lists:
   - Size 1: Direct access to single element
   - Size 2-4: Can store inline in SINGLE/prefix nodes, direct indexed access
3. Pattern-match on list literals in user code to generate direct accesses

Example optimization:
```dark
// Before (what we have)
let list = [1, 2, 3] in
let x = Stdlib.List.getAt(list, 1)  // Full FingerTree dispatch

// After (optimized)
let list = [1, 2, 3] in
let x = 2  // Constant folded, or direct RawGet if list escapes
```

## Files to modify
- `src/anf_optimization.rs` - Add list size tracking and getAt specialization
- `src/stdlib/list.dark` - Add specialized variants for small lists
- `src/type_info.rs` - Track list size as type property (refinement type)

## Impact
Estimated 2-5x speedup for small list access patterns, which are common in functional code.

title: Fix list literal deep-node construction for 6+ elements
description: Revert the pushBack-based list literal fix and implement proper FingerTree DEEP construction (including middle nodes) for list literals with 6+ elements. Add e2e tests for 6-50 element lists across ints/bools/strings/records and ensure they pass.
close_reason: Implemented DEEP middle-spine construction for list literals; added 6–50 element list tests for ints/bools/strings/records; list-filter tests pass.

title: Optimization: Tail Call Loop Parameter Shuffling
description:
## Summary
The tail call is correctly detected and converted to a loop, but the parameter shuffling for each iteration generates excessive moves.

## Evidence from ANF (after Tail Call Detection)
```
let TempId 14 = TailCall(leibnizLoop, [t11, t1, t12, t13])
```

Tail call is correctly detected. However, the conversion to a loop in MIR generates unnecessary parameter copying because all 4 parameters are treated as needing shuffling, even when some don't depend on each other.

## Root Cause
1. The tail-call to loop conversion treats all parameters uniformly
2. Parameters that can be computed in-place still get copied through temporaries
3. Register coalescing doesn't align loop variables with parameters

## Impact
Estimated 10-15% performance improvement

## Implementation Approach
1. Detect when tail-call parameters can be computed in-place
2. For parameters that don't depend on each other, allow direct assignment
3. Use register coalescing preferences to make loop variables and parameters share registers

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/3_ANF_to_MIR.fs - Tail call to loop conversion
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/5_RegisterAllocation.fs - Coalescing preferences

## Benchmark Reference
See: docs/investigations/benchmark-leibniz-optimization.md

Leak report prints to stderr when >0. Updated codegen/layout/binary flags + CLI/test wiring; added leak_check e2e test. Tests: ./run-tests --filter=leak_check; ./run-tests

title: Optimization: Unboxed Option type to eliminate heap allocation
description:
## Problem
Every `getAt` call allocates a 16-byte Option wrapper on the heap, even when the result is immediately destructured.

## Evidence from LIR dump (matmul benchmark)
The `getAt_i64` function has 6 separate `HeapAlloc(16)` calls for Option construction:

```
Label "Stdlib.FingerTree.getAt_i64_L9":
  v11356 <- HeapAlloc(16)
  HeapStore(v11356, 0, Imm 0)      ; discriminant = Some (0)
  HeapStore(v11356, 8, Reg v11355) ; payload
  v11357 <- Mov(Reg v11356)

Label "Stdlib.FingerTree.getAt_i64_L10":
  v11360 <- HeapAlloc(16)
  HeapStore(v11360, 0, Imm 1)      ; discriminant = None (1)  
  HeapStore(v11360, 8, Imm 0)      ; unused payload
```

In `matGet`, the Option is immediately destructured:
```
v11323 <- Call(Stdlib.FingerTree.getAt_i64, ...)
v11325 <- HeapLoad(v11323, 0)    ; load discriminant
Cmp(v11325, Imm 0)               ; is Some?
Branch...
v11334 <- HeapLoad(v11323, 8)    ; load payload
```

## Solution
1. Use tagged pointers for `Option<Int64>`: null = None, non-null points to value
2. Or use register pairs (discriminant in one reg, value in another) for return
3. Or implement unboxed option optimization: return convention uses sentinel value

For `Option<Int64>`:
- Return 0x1 (unaligned pointer) for None
- Return actual pointer to boxed value for Some
- Or use two-register return: X0=discriminant, X1=value

## Files to modify
- `src/types.rs` - Add unboxed representation for Option<T>
- `src/lir_codegen.rs` - Generate inline Option handling
- `src/calling_convention.rs` - Two-register return for Option

## Impact
Estimated 10-20% speedup on code using Option types (which is extensive due to getAt returning Option).

title: Optimization: Inline constant tag functions for FingerTree
description:
## Problem
Functions like `__TAG_SINGLE`, `__TAG_DEEP`, `__TAG_LEAF` return constant values but are called via full function call overhead in the FingerTree implementation.

## Evidence from LIR dump (matmul benchmark)
```
Stdlib.FingerTree.__TAG_SINGLE:
  Label "Stdlib.FingerTree.__TAG_SINGLE_body":
    X0 <- Mov(Imm 1)
    Ret

Stdlib.FingerTree.__TAG_DEEP:
  Label "Stdlib.FingerTree.__TAG_DEEP_body":
    X0 <- Mov(Imm 2)
    Ret
```

These are called repeatedly during list access:
```
v11330 <- Call(Stdlib.FingerTree.__TAG_SINGLE, [])
RestoreRegs([], [])
v11330 <- Mov(Reg X0)
Cmp(v11328, Reg v11330)
```

Each call involves SaveRegs/RestoreRegs overhead even though the result is always a constant.

## Solution
1. Mark these functions with an `@inline` or `@const` annotation
2. Add constant propagation pass that replaces `Call(__TAG_SINGLE)` with `Imm 1`
3. Apply this optimization in ANF optimization phase

## Files to modify
- `src/anf_optimization.rs` - Add constant function detection
- `src/stdlib/finger_tree.dark` or equivalent - Mark tag functions as const

## Impact
Estimated 5-10% speedup on list-heavy code.

title: Optimization: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) in ANF
description:
## Summary
Add Common Subexpression Elimination pass to ANF optimization pipeline to eliminate redundant computations.

## Impact Estimate
15-25% performance improvement on primes benchmark.

## Root Cause
The ANF transformation produces duplicate computations that are not eliminated. For example, `guess + 1` is computed 3 times in the same branch:

### Evidence from Dark ANF:
```
let TempId 5 = t1 + 1           ; first computation
let TempId 6 = t1 + 1           ; REDUNDANT - same as t5
let TempId 7 = t5 * t6
...
let TempId 9 = t1 + 1           ; REDUNDANT - third time
```

Should become:
```
let TempId 5 = t1 + 1           ; computed once
let TempId 7 = t5 * t5          ; reuse t5
let TempId 10 = TailCall(isqrt, [t0, t5])  ; reuse t5
```

### Evidence from Generated Assembly (countPrimes):
```asm
countPrimes_L12:
    sub x3, x21, #1             ; n - 1
    add x21, x20, #1            ; count + 1
    mov x20, x3
    mov x19, x21
    ldur x16, [x29, #-104]      ; STACK ACCESS - unnecessary
    stur x16, [x29, #-96]       ; STACK ACCESS - unnecessary
    mov x4, x3                  ; REDUNDANT MOV
    mov x3, x21                 ; REDUNDANT MOV
    mov x21, x20                ; REDUNDANT MOV
    mov x20, x19                ; REDUNDANT MOV
    b countPrimes_body
```

Compare to Rust (same logic):
```asm
8418:   add x8, x8, #1          ; count++
841c:   cmp x11, x10            ; bounds check
8420:   mov x12, x11            ; one register move
8424:   b.ge 847c               ; branch
```

## Implementation Approach
1. Add CSE pass after ANF conversion
2. Build hash table of expressions by (operation, operand1, operand2)
3. When encountering duplicate expression, replace with reference to first computation
4. Handle commutative operations (a + b == b + a)

## Files to Modify
- `src/anf/optimizations/mod.rs` - Add CSE pass to optimization pipeline
- `src/anf/optimizations/cse.rs` - New file for CSE implementation

## Reference
See full investigation: docs/investigations/benchmark-primes-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Improve Register Allocation Around Function Calls
description:
## Summary
Reduce excessive register spilling around function calls by better utilizing callee-saved registers.

## Impact Estimate
10-20% performance improvement.

## Root Cause
The register allocator spills many registers around function calls even when callee-saved registers (X19-X28) are available.

### Evidence from Dark LIR (before register allocation):
```
Label "countPrimes_L16":
    SaveRegs([X1, X2, X3, X4, X5, X6, X7], [])  ; Save 7 registers!
    ArgMoves(X0 <- Reg X21, X1 <- Imm 1)
    v10100 <- Call(isqrt, ...)
    RestoreRegs([X1, X2, X3, X4, X5, X6, X7], [])  ; Restore 7 registers!
```

### Evidence from Generated Assembly:
```asm
countPrimes_L16:
    sub sp, sp, #0x50           ; allocate stack frame
    stp x1, x2, [sp]            ; spill to stack
    stp x3, x4, [sp, #16]       ; spill
    stp x5, x6, [sp, #32]       ; spill
    str x7, [sp, #48]           ; spill
    ... call ...
    ldp x1, x2, [sp]            ; restore from stack
    ldp x3, x4, [sp, #16]       ; restore
    ldp x5, x6, [sp, #32]       ; restore
    ldr x7, [sp, #48]           ; restore
    add sp, sp, #0x50           ; deallocate
```

### Rust comparison - efficient register usage:
```asm
; No spills around division/modulo - values stay in callee-saved registers
8464:   udiv x15, x12, x14
8468:   msub x16, x15, x14, x12
846c:   cinc x14, x14, lt
; Loop continues with no stack access
```

### The `countPrimes_body` has 22 phi nodes, indicating:
- Excessive variable copies during SSA construction
- Poor live range management

## Implementation Approach
1. Prioritize callee-saved registers (X19-X28) for values that live across calls
2. Split live ranges at call sites rather than spilling
3. Reduce live range of temporaries used only within basic blocks
4. Consider rematerialization for cheap-to-compute values

## Files to Modify
- `src/codegen/register_allocator.rs` - Improve allocation strategy
- `src/lir/` - Better live range analysis
- Consider linear scan vs graph coloring tradeoffs

## Reference
See full investigation: docs/investigations/benchmark-primes-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Post-RA Copy Propagation for MOV Chains
description:
## Summary
Add a post-register-allocation copy propagation pass to eliminate redundant MOV instruction chains.

## Problem
After register allocation, chains of MOV instructions exist that could be simplified. For example:
```
X21 <- Mov(Reg X0)
X24 <- Mov(Reg X21)   ; Could be X24 <- Mov(Reg X0)
```

## Evidence from tak Benchmark
The tak function entry has these redundant moves after PHI lowering:
```
Label "tak_entry":
    X21 <- Mov(Reg X0)
    X20 <- Mov(Reg X1)
    X19 <- Mov(Reg X2)
    X22 <- Mov(Reg X19)   ; REDUNDANT - should be X22 <- X2
    X23 <- Mov(Reg X20)   ; REDUNDANT - should be X23 <- X1
    X24 <- Mov(Reg X21)   ; REDUNDANT - should be X24 <- X0
```

Compare with Rust which uses only 4 total MOV instructions in the entry path.

## Solution
When `Rd <- Rs` followed by `Rx <- Rd` where Rd is not used later:
1. Replace with `Rx <- Rs`
2. Eliminate the dead intermediate MOV

This can be implemented as a peephole pass after register allocation but before code generation.

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/4.5_LIR_Optimize.fs - Add cross-block copy propagation

## Estimated Impact
3-5% speedup on recursive functions like tak

title: Optimization: Constant Branch Elimination in MIR
description:
## Summary
Eliminate branches on constant boolean values and remove resulting dead code.

## Impact Estimate
10-15% performance improvement.

## Root Cause
After inlining `isPrime` into `countPrimes`, the compiler produces code that branches on literal booleans, generating unreachable code:

### Evidence from Dark ANF (after inlining):
```
if t42 then
    let TempId 30 = false       ; Constant false
    if t30 then                 ; DEAD BRANCH - always takes else
        ...                     ; unreachable code generated anyway
    else
        ...
else
    let TempId 30 = true        ; Constant true
    if t30 then                 ; DEAD BRANCH - always takes then
        ...
    else
        ...                     ; unreachable code generated anyway
```

### Evidence from Generated LIR:
```
Label "countPrimes_L21":
    v10112 <- Mov(Imm 0)
    v1002 <- Mov(Imm 0)
    Branch(v1002, Label "countPrimes_L24", Label "countPrimes_L25")  ; Always L25
Label "countPrimes_L22":
    v10102 <- Mov(Imm 1)
    v1002 <- Mov(Imm 1)
    Branch(v1002, Label "countPrimes_L27", Label "countPrimes_L28")  ; Always L27
```

### Result:
Dark `countPrimes` has 30 basic blocks (L0-L29), many unreachable.
Rust has the entire logic in ~15 instructions with no dead paths.

## Implementation Approach
1. In MIR optimization, detect `Branch(const, ...)` patterns
2. Replace with unconditional `Jump` to the appropriate target
3. Run DCE (dead code elimination) to remove unreachable blocks
4. Consider also handling at LIR level for constant loads followed by branch

## Files to Modify
- `src/mir/optimizations/const_prop.rs` - Add constant branch elimination
- `src/mir/optimizations/dce.rs` - Mark/sweep unreachable blocks

## Reference
See full investigation: docs/investigations/benchmark-primes-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Induction Variable Optimization / Loop Strength Reduction
description:
## Summary
The leibniz benchmark shows Dark computes `2*i+1` every iteration instead of maintaining a derived counter. Rust/LLVM optimizes this to a counter that starts at 1 and increments by 2.

## Evidence from Dark ANF
```
let TempId 6 = t0 << 1     // 2 * i
let TempId 7 = t6 + 1      // 2 * i + 1
```

This generates 2 integer operations per iteration that could be eliminated.

## Evidence from Rust Assembly (7 instructions total)
```asm
9378: ucvtf d2, x8        // convert counter to float (counter = 2*i+1)
937c: add x8, x8, #0x2    // increment counter by 2 (next odd number)
9380: cmp x8, x9          // compare with limit
9384: fdiv d2, d1, d2     // sign / divisor
9388: fneg d1, d1         // sign = -sign
938c: fadd d0, d0, d2     // sum += term
9390: b.ne 9378           // loop
```

LLVM maintains `2*i+1` as a derived counter that increments by 2 each iteration.

## Root Cause
Missing induction variable detection and optimization pass.

## Impact
Estimated 15-20% performance improvement

## Implementation Approach
Add an induction variable detection pass in `3.5_MIR_Optimize.fs` that:
1. Detects linear induction variables (expressions of the form `a*i + b` where `i` is the loop counter)
2. Creates a derived induction variable for each such expression
3. Initializes the derived variable before the loop
4. Increments it by `a` (the coefficient) each iteration instead of recomputing

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/3.5_MIR_Optimize.fs - Add induction variable optimization

## References
- Classic compiler optimization, see "Engineering a Compiler" Chapter 10
- LLVM's ScalarEvolution pass
- docs/investigations/benchmark-leibniz-optimization.md

title: Optimization: Add Array type with O(1) access
description:
## Problem
Dark lacks native array support, forcing use of FingerTree-based Lists which have O(log n) access complexity vs O(1) for arrays. This is the single largest performance gap between Dark and Rust/OCaml on numerical benchmarks.

## Evidence from matmul benchmark

**Rust inner loop** - Single instruction for array access:
```asm
9934: ldr x1, [x1, x3, lsl #3]  ; a[i][k] - one instruction, O(1)
9938: ldr x5, [x5, x13, lsl #3] ; b[k][j] - one instruction, O(1)
9948: madd x17, x5, x1, x17     ; fused multiply-add
```

**Dark getAt** - Full tree traversal:
```
Stdlib.FingerTree.getAt_i64:
  Cmp(v551, Imm 0)           ; negative index check
  Branch...
  And_imm(v550, #7)          ; extract tag (3 bits)
  Call(__TAG_SINGLE)         ; compare tags
  Cmp(v11328, Reg v11330)
  Branch...
  Call(__TAG_DEEP)
  ; ... 17 labels total, multiple function calls
  ; O(log n) tree traversal
```

The Dark benchmark actually uses 3x3 matrices (hardcoded) instead of 100x100 due to "nested list bugs", suggesting even the FingerTree approach has issues at scale.

## Solution
Add a native `Array<T>` type with:
1. Contiguous memory layout (like Rust Vec)
2. O(1) indexed access: `Array.get(arr, idx)` compiles to single load
3. Bounds checking can be optimized away when index is provably in bounds

Example generated code for `Array.get`:
```asm
; arr in X0, idx in X1, result in X0
cmp X1, [X0, #8]        ; bounds check against length
b.ge panic_oob
ldr X0, [X0, X1, lsl #3] ; load element
```

## Files to modify
- `src/parser.rs` - Add Array type syntax
- `src/types.rs` - Array type definition
- `src/stdlib/array.dark` - Array operations (get, set, length, map, etc.)
- `src/lir_codegen.rs` - O(1) array access codegen
- `src/mir/bounds_check.rs` - Optional bounds check elimination

## Impact
Estimated 10-100x speedup for array-heavy benchmarks like matmul. This is the most impactful optimization for numerical workloads.

## Additional context
The benchmark note "Uses hardcoded small matrices due to nested list bugs" suggests the FingerTree list implementation may have correctness issues with nested structures that should also be investigated.

title: Optimization: Dead Move Chain Elimination
description:
## Summary
Eliminate intermediate registers that are defined by MOV and only used by a single subsequent MOV.

## Problem
After PHI lowering, intermediate registers are created that only serve to pass values to another register. These add instruction overhead without benefit.

## Evidence from tak Benchmark
```
X21 <- Mov(Reg X0)   ; X21 is only used by next line
X24 <- Mov(Reg X21)  ; X24 is the actual live value
```

X21 is defined but only ever used as a source for X24. This is equivalent to:
```
X24 <- Mov(Reg X0)   ; Direct move, X21 eliminated
```

## Solution
1. After register allocation, perform a use-def analysis
2. For each MOV Rd <- Rs:
   - If Rd is used exactly once, and that use is another MOV Rx <- Rd
   - And Rd is not otherwise used (no reads between definition and final use)
   - Then: eliminate the intermediate, replace with Rx <- Rs
3. This is a form of dead store elimination specific to MOV chains

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/4.5_LIR_Optimize.fs - Add dead move chain elimination pass

## Estimated Impact
1-2% speedup on code with PHI nodes (loops, tail recursion)

title: Optimization: Loop-invariant code motion for repeated list access
description:
## Problem
In patterns like matrix access, the same row is accessed multiple times with different column indices. Each access repeats the row lookup.

## Evidence from matmul benchmark ANF

In the `dot3` function, same matrix row accessed 3 times:
```
Function dot3:
let TempId 600 = matGet(t596, t598, 0)  ; a[i][0] - row i lookup
let TempId 601 = matGet(t597, 0, t599)  ; b[0][j]
let TempId 602 = t600 * t601
let TempId 603 = matGet(t596, t598, 1)  ; a[i][1] - SAME row i lookup!
let TempId 604 = matGet(t597, 1, t599)  ; b[1][j]
let TempId 605 = t603 * t604
let TempId 606 = t602 + t605
let TempId 607 = matGet(t596, t598, 2)  ; a[i][2] - SAME row i again!
let TempId 608 = matGet(t597, 2, t599)  ; b[2][j]
```

Each `matGet` does:
1. `Stdlib.List.getAt_list_i64(mat, row)` - O(log n) to get row
2. `Stdlib.List.getAt_i64(row, col)` - O(log n) to get element

The row lookup (step 1) is invariant for all accesses with same `i`, but is repeated 3 times.

## Solution
1. Implement loop-invariant code motion (LICM) pass in ANF optimization
2. Detect when first argument to `getAt` is invariant across multiple calls
3. Hoist the row lookup, reuse the result for column accesses

Optimized form:
```
let row_i = Stdlib.List.getAt_list_i64(t596, t598)  ; hoist row lookup
let TempId 600 = Stdlib.List.getAt_i64(row_i, 0)   ; reuse row
let TempId 603 = Stdlib.List.getAt_i64(row_i, 1)   ; reuse row  
let TempId 607 = Stdlib.List.getAt_i64(row_i, 2)   ; reuse row
```

This halves the FingerTree traversals for matrix access patterns.

## Files to modify
- `src/anf_optimization.rs` - Add LICM pass for list access patterns
- `src/analysis/invariant.rs` - Invariant detection for nested data access

## Impact
Estimated 1.5-2x speedup in matrix-like nested list access patterns. Lower priority because Array type (c4d-i4x) would make this less relevant.

title: Optimization: MADD Instruction Fusion
description:
## Summary
Add peephole optimization to fuse MUL+ADD sequences into ARM64 MADD instructions.

## Impact
~10-15% performance improvement for numerical code

## Evidence
Dark computes `a * b + c` as separate instructions:
```
let TempId 5 = t2 * t4          // ij * (ij + 1)
let TempId 7 = t6 + t0          // + i
```

Rust LLVM fuses into single MADD:
```asm
9b0c318c    madd x12, x12, x12, x12   // x12 = x12*x12 + x12
```

The spectral_norm hot loop uses this pattern extensively for the matrix index calculation `(i+j)*(i+j+1)/2 + i + 1`.

## Implementation Approach
Add peephole optimization to detect:
- `t1 <- Mul(a, b)` followed by `t2 <- Add(t1, c)` where t1 is only used by t2
- Replace with `t2 <- Madd(a, b, c)`

## Files to Modify
- `src/DarkCompiler/ARM64.fs` - Add MADD instruction encoding
- `src/DarkCompiler/passes/6_CodeGen.fs` - Peephole for MADD fusion
- `src/DarkCompiler/LIR.fs` - Add MADD LIR instruction

## Related Investigation
docs/investigations/benchmark-spectral_norm-optimization.md

title: Cleanup tests to use stdout much less
description: A lot of the tests have their expected value as something like `= stdout="5.50\n"`. However, the test suites nearly supports a simpler version: `= 5.50`. The difference is the newline. I'd like to allow the test definition so that we can write `= 5.50` instead of the longer stdout-based test definition. Change the test parser to allow newline by default, then change all the tests to use the shorter, cleaner test definition

title: Optimization: Eliminate redundant isEmpty/head checks in list operations
description:
## Optimization: Eliminate Redundant List Checks

**Impact Estimate:** 10-15% improvement

### Problem

The filterHelper performs redundant checks:

```
Function Stdlib.List.__filterHelper_i64:
let TempId 1181 = Stdlib.FingerTree.isEmpty_i64(t1178)    ; Check 1: isEmpty
if t1181 then return t1180
else
    let TempId 1182 = Stdlib.FingerTree.head_i64(t1178)   ; Check 2: head returns Option
    let TempId 1183 = t1182.0
    let TempId 1184 = t1183 == 1                          ; Check 3: is None?
```

After confirming `!isEmpty`, the `head` call still returns an Option and checks for None - this is redundant.

### Evidence

The pattern `isEmpty + head + None-check` appears in:
- `filterHelper`
- `reverseHelper`
- `checksumHelper`
- Many other list operations

Each redundant check adds:
- 1 function call to `isEmpty`
- Tag extraction and comparison
- Branch instruction

**OCaml/Rust approach:**
```ocaml
| [] -> acc                    (* Single pattern match handles both *)
| x :: xs -> ...
```

```rust
for x in arr.iter() { ... }    // Iterator guarantees valid element
```

### Implementation Approach

1. **Introduce `headTail` operation**
   - Returns `Option<(head, tail)>` in single operation
   - Eliminates separate isEmpty check

2. **Pattern match optimization**
   - Recognize `if isEmpty then ... else head/tail` pattern
   - Convert to single destructuring operation

### Files to Modify

- `src/stdlib/finger_tree.dark` - Add headTail operation
- `src/anf/optimizations/pattern_match.rs` - Optimize isEmpty+head pattern

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] headTail operation available for lists
- [ ] List iteration uses single check instead of isEmpty + head + None-check
- [ ] filterHelper uses optimized pattern

title: Benchmarks: mandelbrot validation mismatch
description: The benchmark run reports mandelbrot validation mismatch: expected 15909, got 24091. Investigate compiler/runtime behavior for the benchmark and update expected output or fix the bug.
close_reason: Fixed. The 'validation mismatch' was not a compiler bug - the Dark mandelbrot code was counting escaped points (24091) while the Rust reference counts points in the set (15909). Swapped the return values to match Rust semantics. Now outputs 15909 and passes validation.

title: Optimization: Improved PHI Coalescing for Entry Block Sources
description:
## Summary
Improve PHI node coalescing to prioritize register sharing between PHI destinations and their entry-block sources.

## Problem
PHI nodes with entry block operands (function arguments) don't share registers with their sources, causing extra MOV instructions.

## Evidence from tak Benchmark
```
tak_body:
    v10018 <- Phi([(Reg v2, tak_entry), (Reg v10030, tak_L1)])      ; z
    v10019 <- Phi([(Reg v1, tak_entry), (Reg v10029, tak_L1)])      ; y
    v10020 <- Phi([(Reg v0, tak_entry), (Reg v10028, tak_L1)])      ; x
```

After register allocation:
- v2 (argument z) gets X19, but v10018 gets X22, requiring a MOV
- v1 (argument y) gets X20, but v10019 gets X23, requiring a MOV
- v0 (argument x) gets X21, but v10020 gets X24, requiring a MOV

## Solution
1. In the register allocator, prioritize coalescing PHI destinations with their entry-block sources
2. Entry block operands are "free" - they come from argument registers that we control
3. When a PHI operand comes from the entry block, try hard to allocate the PHI destination to the same register

The current `collectPhiPreferences` function in RegisterAllocation.fs builds a preference graph but doesn't weight entry block sources higher.

## Files to Modify
- src/DarkCompiler/passes/5_RegisterAllocation.fs - Enhance collectPhiPreferences to prioritize entry block sources

## Estimated Impact
2-4% speedup on recursive functions with tail call optimization

title: List literal 6+ elements builds unused LEAF nodes
description: List literal lowering allocates LEAF nodes even when count > 5 and then rebuilds list via pushBack. Those LEAF allocations are not referenced and still execute, which is wasted allocation and likely a leak with RC. Skip createLeaves for count > 5 or defer to count <= 5 path. See src/DarkCompiler/passes/2_AST_to_ANF.fs around list literal lowering.

comments.author: Paul Biggar
comments.text: Added ANF optimization test for 6-element list literal to avoid unused leaf allocs and updated list literal lowering to skip createLeaves when count > 5, building via pushBack with only element bindings. Attempted ./run-tests --filter=optimization but build failed due to permission on /home/paulbiggar/.nuget/NuGet.

title: TypeApp return types not instantiated for lambda capture inference
description:
## Plan

**Problem**: `simpleInferType` at 2_AST_to_ANF.fs:920-922 looks up generic function return types from `funcReturnTypes` but doesn't apply type argument substitution. When `identity<Int64>(42)` is called, the inferred type is `TVar "T"` instead of `TInt64`, causing lambda captures to store wrong types.

**Root Cause**: TypeApp case ignores typeArgs:
```fsharp
| AST.TypeApp (funcName, _, _) ->  // typeArgs ignored!
    Map.tryFind funcName funcReturnTypes
```

**Fix Approach**:
1. Fix `applySubstToType` to recurse into TSum type args (line 413 returns TSum unchanged - bug)
2. Add `GenericFuncDefs: Map<string, string list * AST.Type>` to LiftState (TypeParams * ReturnType)
3. Populate from both user FunctionDefs AND stdlib ModuleFuncs
4. In TypeApp case: build substitution, apply to return type

**Key Files**:
- Bug: src/DarkCompiler/passes/2_AST_to_ANF.fs:920-922
- LiftState: same file, lines 811-816
- applySubstToType: same file, lines 397-414
- Stdlib generics: src/DarkCompiler/Stdlib.fs (ModuleFunc.TypeParams)

**Edge Cases**:
- Multiple type params: `pair<A,B>` returning `(A, B)`
- Nested types: `TList(TVar "T")` with typeArg `Int64`
- TSum return types: `Option<T>` needs recursive substitution
- Stdlib generics: `__raw_get<v>`, `__list_empty<a>` etc.

**Acceptance**:
- [ ] Test: lambda capturing result of generic call compiles correctly
- [ ] applySubstToType handles TSum recursively
- [ ] Both user and stdlib generics instantiated properly
- [ ] No regression in existing closure/generics tests

title: Add failing test for lambda capture of generic call
description: Add test in src/Tests/e2e/generics.e2e that captures result of identity<Int64>(42) in a lambda. Should fail until fix is complete.

title: Fix applySubstToType to handle TSum recursively
description: At 2_AST_to_ANF.fs:413, TSum case returns unchanged. Fix to recurse into type args: TSum(name, List.map (applySubstToType subst) args)

title: Add GenericFuncDefs to LiftState
description: Add GenericFuncDefs: Map<string, string list * AST.Type> to LiftState (lines 811-816). Populate from user FunctionDefs (extractGenericFuncDefs line 319) AND stdlib ModuleFuncs with TypeParams.

title: Fix TypeApp case in simpleInferType
description: At 2_AST_to_ANF.fs:920-922, change to: look up funcName in GenericFuncDefs, build substitution from TypeParams+typeArgs, apply to return type. Fall back to funcReturnTypes for non-generic functions.

title: Verify all tests pass
description: Run full test suite. Ensure new test passes, existing closures.e2e and generics.e2e tests still pass, no regressions.
